sentences.text: Good morning, Sophia.
Yes, I was born in Hanaczow in 1922.
Poland.
My name is Jennie, J-E-N-N-I-E, Sauer, S-A-U-E-R.
And my maiden-- maiden name is Braver, B-R-A-V-E-R.
Yisrael.
We called him, I mean, Yisrael Braver.
Mina Braver.
Huss.
H-U-S-S.
He was in business with my, it was a family business with my grandfather, And was sort of like an-- how would they say, [? lumber ?] and wood?
I-- I don't know how to explain.
They used to buy oak and cut it down and make materials for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for beer barrels.
And my father was a part of it, my grandfather and my uncles.
Harmatz.
My grandfather, right.
In my immediate family?
There were-- we were four children.
I was the youngest.
There are three brothers and I.
My brother, the oldest was Yizaak.
And the next one was Eli-- [?
Eliyahu, ?] Eli.
And then Herman.
Yes, my mother's sister.
Her name was Feiga Huss.
So you want me to spell it?
Feiga is F-E-I-G-A and last name was H-U-S-S.
It was a village.
It was a very pretty village.
And they weren't too many, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were quite, I mean Jewish families, they were maybe about 30.
And it was very nice, a very-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I felt, you know, sort of very-- it was a happy time.
Of course, I was young.
It was, you know, childhood, and-- and we lived there from-- until the-- until the war broke out.
Yes.
To a public school, and also we had like a private, you know, like a cheder, you know, a private tutoring, Hebrew, Jewish, Polish, so different, you know.
It was pretty good, certain times.
But they were times that it wasn't so pleasant, when it came like Christmas time and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like Easter time.
So they were very agitated, the-- the Polish.
They were, you know, Catholics.
And it wasn't a pleasant feeling for Jewish kids that they used to exclude us from, you know, from any plays that they had.
And-- and of course, they had religious [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hours that they used to, you know.
So we had to to leave the classroom and so.
So we were outside playing, you know, like a little recess.
They didn't say much, but they were sort of very distant.
And they always felt that we had to do with, you know, Jesus, that we get, you know, the Jewish-- Jews, you know, had to do with it.
So I-- I guess it was sort of, it was anti-Semitic.
But during the-- otherwise, they were pretty nice.
They were sort of, you know, friendly.
But mostly, on these holidays, they were very, you know, like they weren't too friendly.
I would say, I would-- at that time, I guess we all, you know, we observed all the holidays.
And we didn't ride on Shabbos, and we didn't do any things, you know.
I recall, you know, that we were sort of very observant.
And we weren't orthodox orthodox, you know, like.
But very observant, very, you know, and to the Jewish traditions and kosher and all that.
You mean like holidays?
What?
Or Shabbat.
My father used to go to shul.
And of course, we came back.
We had a nice festive lunch.
And I didn't do nothing, just relaxed and you know.
Friends and relatives used to come together in the afternoon, and visit, and spend a nice day together.
Just one.
It was a house, a little house.
And it wasn't anything special, but it's just like a house for worship.
And also next to this, you know, to the synagogue was this place that we used to have the cheder, you know, to go to Jewish school there.
It depends.
I mean, when it came close to the-- I guess there was a time that we realized that there's a lot going on.
And my father used to have, you know-- how would I say?
The Jewish paper always, you know, was sent from-- from [POLISH] or Lwow.
And he knew exactly what-- we knew what's going on and it was.
And of course, we had read the other paper.
We knew that there was very, you know, things weren't good.
And of course, the-- the people that were surrounded, the Polish people, you know, there was anti-Semitism.
We have a feeling of things are not, we are not, you know, liked.
And things started getting worse and worse.
Some were distant.
And they always felt that the Jewish people are in business and they-- they make money.
And the things are taken away from them.
They always blamed everything, you know.
The Jewish people lived a little bit sort of a, a more, a nicer life.
So they always felt that we are taking away from them.
I guess that was the feeling.
And they just resented us.
Yeah, yes.
The neighbors were very nice because I guess we were people that we were getting along very nice with them.
And they liked us.
And it used to be like, you know, with the Gentiles, they didn't like in general the-- the-- the Jews.
But there are certain Jews, like neighbors, and when they-- they were getting along nice, so they liked us.
They-- they didn't have anything against.
Before the things happened, not really.
There was no place to go.
There was no place.
I mean, we had a lot of relatives here in the States.
But it wasn't easy to leave.
And it was a quota and it wasn't easy to leave.
But there was no way of talking and going someplace.
There was no.
This was the summer, you know, before the-- the Germans, you know, attacked Poland.
We knew things are not good.
And we just didn't know what to do, where to go, how to handle it.
We were just like trapped, and we had to stay and wait what's going to happen.
So what happened it's in September, I think it was, that they attacked Poland.
They just, they took over, where they just marched in, and they took over Poland.
I mean, there was a few days' fights, but there was no way to--
The Germans, yeah, and this was in 1939.
They marched in, and if I remember right, there was just a very short time.
I-- I can't tell exactly, two or three weeks.
And then they made some deal with the Russians, so they pulled back, I think, to [NON-ENGLISH] if it's familiar.
And then the Russians marched in.
So we had the Germans for-- for I guess a few weeks.
I-- I can't exactly give you the-- the-- the time.
But then the Germans pulled back, and the Russians moved in.
And we were under the Russians till 1941.
It wasn't good, but the same time, I thought-- we thought it was better than the Germans.
It was very bad, because, you know, they, you know, they took things away.
And they-- they were very poor, and they just, you know, they took everything.
And we were just, you know, surviving.
But still they didn't threaten us with killing or things like that.
But times weren't good, and we still had a feeling some-- some other things are going to come forward again.
We weren't relaxed, though.
We were very frightened by it.
I remember when going back when before the Germans came in, a few days before, the planes were just cruising.
And it was such a terrible thing in the air and was such a frightening feeling.
We were just, we were running around like, you know, didn't know what to do with ourselves.
But then when they pulled back and the Russians came in, was like a relief.
So it was better, but still we-- we lived under-- under constant fear.
And we knew things are going to, other things are going to come up.
We didn't know what, but it was a-- a bad feeling.
No, it was very little, was very little.
They took over all the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, everything.
They, it was like under their, you know, the government took over everything.
And they just, you know, let us have a little bit.
But of course, in our place, it was a village.
So we had our gardens and things.
So we tried to have a little bit something that we didn't have to depend just, you know, on them.
But it was not, there wasn't enough food.
No, no, no, no.
There was nothing.
There was just everything was like a very unstable time.
There was-- there was nothing going on.
It was sort of like we were trapped and we just didn't do nothing.
We just waited what the future's going to bring.
Just around home and just talking to-- to friends, to neighbors, what-- what to expect.
Everybody was in the same position.
Everybody was very frightened.
And-- but we were hoping, maybe some miracle is going to happen.
To what?
I'm sorry.
No, there was no place to go.
There was no, just a minute.
Let me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, there was no place to go.
Then in 1941, the Germans, I think it was in June or July, so then they attacked the-- the-- the Russians.
And they started the war with Russians.
And then when we went through the same thing, the planes were, you know, it was-- it was like a-- a war in the air, you know.
And that time, some people start to-- to-- to run, to go like with the Russians.
So in fact, an uncle of my and my middle brother, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because there are no cars and no things, just horse and buggies and all this, they packed a lot of things.
And they said they're going to Russia.
But usually the men, because they-- they always felt that the woman are more safe, you know, when-- when the Germans are going to come.
And so the men run away.
So this uncle, he lived in Lwow.
But he came that time to Hanaczow.
And he dealed a lot that time before the war with the Germans.
He-- he always felt that the Germans cannot do things like that, because he used to export, you know, this-- you know, the materials for the barrels to-- to Germany.
And he felt that the people that couldn't, you know, he was sort of friendly with them.
But so they, so he left anyway.
And they went, he, and my brother, and I think a few other men.
And they went till I guess [POLISH], which was close to the Russian border.
And then they saw what's going on there.
And-- and he left his wife and children and this.
And he turned back, and he came back.
He said, now, things cannot be as bad as-- as-- as they are, you know, as they talk about.
But so he came back, yeah.
But not too many people run away, no.
But my-- my brother was taken.
He was in the army, so he was taken back to-- to Russia, you know.
He was in the Russian Army at that time-- no, he was in the Polish-- let's see.
Oh, it's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, that time the Russian took over, so he was in the Russian Army.
So he was-- you know, when they pulled back, he went back with them.
But we didn't know about it till the end.
The youngest.
Herman.
Next-- so the German came in, and they took over.
It was just, it was a terrible thing.
We were going around just, we didn't know what to do.
And-- and being that this was a village, you know, this wasn't a big city like Lwow.
They had the Germans came in, they took over.
And-- but being the-- the smaller towns or villages, they gave over the-- the-- the power to the Ukrainians.
And they were the ones that, they were just awful.
So we were-- they were the militia, police, you know.
And they, you know, had the-- the authority over us.
And they were just terrible.
They used to come and they used to-- to beat us up.
They used to take us, you know, to forced labor every day and take us out in-- in the fields there and dig ditches.
And they used to stay with-- with rifles, and used to have fun.
And they used to, you know, shoot in the air and they used to.
And-- and we had to stay and dig ditches there from-- from about 8 o'clock in the morning till 6 o'clock in the evening.
And this was just the beginning when they just marched in.
And this was going on until it got cold, you know.
I remember it was during the High Holidays.
I remember Erev Yom Kippur, we-- we came home, you know, after digging these ditches and not much to eat.
And then, you know, also we made up, you know, sort of like to pray, to-- to fast like two-- two days a week, we used to fast.
Monday and Thursday we used to fast just for God to perform some miracles for us.
But being young, sometimes I just wonder how things, you know, being young.
It seems that the power of-- of-- when you're frightened, you want to live, you know, that I guess it's mind over matter.
You get strong and, you know, even nothing to eat and nothing to-- I mean, very little.
But somehow, we pulled through.
And this was going on until it got deep into the fall.
And oh, yeah, and of course, you know, after work, you know, we didn't sleep at home.
You know, I used to come home, you know, after this thing, you know.
The-- they didn't take the parents.
They took mostly the younger people to this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] forced labor.
And after coming home, so my mother used to whatever she could prepare a little bit something to eat.
And then we used to leave the house and go in the fields, sleep.
We-- we didn't sleep at home.
Yes, because we were afraid it-- this Ukraine-- you know, they are police.
They used to come during the night.
They used to come any time.
And they had fun.
They had fun torturing us.
They used to tell us to-- to-- to turn-- even during the day, to turn around against the wall.
And they-- they looked around the house, whatever they wanted, they took away.
And they threatened us to kill us and to do that.
And every time I felt that there's going to be a bullet, you know, in back.
But somehow, they used to leave.
But then at night, they used to come.
And so we used to leave the house and sleep in the-- in the fields.
I'm-- and my mother in one place, my brother was another place, my father on the other place.
But then when it got cold, you know, late into the fall, we used to go on the, you know, on the attics, you know.
And they had barns, you know.
It was a village.
They had barns with cows.
But anyway, I used to go there.
And without these neighbors, without the Gentiles-- I mean, some were quite nice.
They didn't want to know.
But we used to climb in there and sleep through the night.
And then early in the morning, come out.
And this was going on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till let's see, this was '41, practically all winter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, and it was-- was a very hard life.
Just not much to eat, not much to-- and cold, and this.
So and then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] towards spring, they start taking-- they start taking us again to-- to forced labor, to take us again, you know.
So by-- by that time, they had already a concentration camp formed very close to our, to-- to Hanaczow, that the neighbors Kurowice.
But they had only man.
No-- no woman.
So that time, they started taking us, this was towards spring.
They're taking us to this camp in the morning.
We used to go-- it was about at least seven miles, I think.
We used to walk there, and work a whole day on the fields.
They had to start preparing like for spring, you know, for planting and things.
And in the evening to go home.
And this was going on the whole summer.
Because they didn't have facilities that time for woman, so they just had men who they used to take out on the-- on the roads to make, you know-- what would I say?

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, the road that was going from there to Lwow, the main, and, you know, to-- you know, knock [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stones into, you know, gravel and things, and make the roads.
So the-- the-- the men used to go there and the-- and the woman from all around, from all these villages used to come in the morning and-- and-- and work there all day and then go home.
So this was going on all summer.
And of course, [INAUDIBLE] and also the same thing the Ukrainians used to come and used to-- they-- they tortured us.
Not that much, though, the Germans didn't come too much in there, because there wasn't enough, you know, Jews to-- to-- to take care.
And they gave it over to them.
And they were much worse in some ways.
They were very-- they were very ignorant people.
They were very, you know, vicious people.
OK.
